exemplar.title: To what extent is there evidence of gender-based price discrimination (pink tax) for personal care products in The Greater Toronto Area?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is there evidence of gender-based price discrimination (pink tax) for personal care products in The Greater Toronto Area?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Topic is introduced immediately and contextualized with statistics, framing a clear and appropriate purpose and focus.
Research question is stated concisely, unambiguously, and maintained consistently throughout the investigation.
Methodology is wellâjustified with an appropriate mixedâmethods approach, effective source selection criteria, and clear linkage to economic theory.
weaknesses: Final sentence in the introduction is incomplete, slightly detracting from overall clarity.
Sampling strategy lacks precise definition (e.g., random or stratified sampling not fully specified).
Minor imprecision around whether âservicesâ fall within the scope of personalâcare goods.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the top band (5â6) descriptors: the topic and purpose are communicated accurately, the research question is clear and tightly focused, and the methodology is appropriate and informed. Minor lapses (incomplete sentence, sampling detail) prevent a deduction, but on balance the criterion is fully satisfied at level 6.

strengths: Economic terminology (e.g., elasticity, consumer surplus, thirdâdegree discrimination) is used accurately and consistently.
Background and diagrams (monopolistic competition, demand elasticity) are clear and support conceptual explanations.
Source material is generally relevant and applied effectively to support arguments.
weaknesses: Explanations occasionally rely on generic textbook descriptions rather than deeper, disciplineâspecific insights.
Some references (e.g., Investopedia, U.S.âcentred sociological claims) lack full academic integration.
Discussion of welfare implications and alternative market structures remains underdeveloped.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates clear and coherent knowledge of the topic and consistently accurate use of terminology, meriting the upper 5â6 band. However, reliance on generic statements and uneven integration of certain sources prevents a fullâmarks award, placing the criterion confidently at level 5.

strengths: Primary data (324 price observations) and secondary studies are consistently appropriate and directly relevant to the research question.
Analysisâincluding Welchâs tâtests and percentage comparisonsâis thorough, effectively grounding conclusions in evidence.
Argument structure is logical and wellâsupported, with a comparative case study of New York City enhancing critical depth.
Limitations are acknowledged, and the investigation maintains a coherent, critical evaluation throughout.
weaknesses: Discussion of causality (e.g., ingredientâcost justifications) is only briefly addressed, leaving gaps in counterargument engagement.
Policy implications, especially the link between U.S. legislative outcomes and the GTA context, remain underdeveloped.
Welfare implications of price discrimination are mentioned but could be explored more deeply to enrich evaluative commentary.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation exhibits research that is consistently appropriate and relevant, effective and wellâsupported analysis, and a wellâstructured argument featuring critical evaluationâcharacteristics of the top band (10â12). Minor gaps in depth of policy and welfare discussion lead to a oneâpoint deduction, yielding a robust score of 11.

strengths: Adheres to IB conventions with a clear title page, table of contents, inâtext citations, bibliography, and numbered pages.
Structure and headings are logical and aid reader navigation.
Layout features (font, spacing, embedding of tables and figures) are consistently applied.
weaknesses: Some figures and tables lack axis labels or units, reducing precision.
Table of contents formatting could be improved (e.g., rightâaligned page numbers, indented subheadings).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets all the conventions at the top of the 3â4 band: structure is clearly appropriate, formatting is correct, and overall readability is high. Only minor formatting refinements are needed, justifying a fullâmarks award of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated in the introduction: âTo what extent is there evidence of gender-based price discrimination (pink tax) for personal care products in Toronto, Canada?â.  It is concise, unambiguous, and tightly focused on a single market and set of products, and the ensuing discussion links back to this question throughout the essay (e.g. data tables, statistical tests, comparison with other regions).  Although minor drift occurs in later policy discussion, the connection between the question and analysis remains clear, justifying a high-band award.

The topic â gender-based price discrimination for personal-care goods in the GTA â is introduced immediately, contextualised with statistics and clearly tied to Economics concepts such as third-degree price discrimination and market segmentation.  The wording and scope are sufficiently narrow (personal-care products, GTA) to be feasible and systematically investigable.  Minor imprecision remains about whether âservicesâ are also included, but overall the purpose and focus are communicated accurately and effectively, matching the 5-6 descriptor.

Source material (scholarly journals, Statistics Canada, NYC DCA, Parsehub, Investopedia) is generally relevant and applied directly to support arguments and comparisons.  However, several references are descriptive or US-centred without deep integration, and some Investopedia articles are more explanatory than academic.  Overall application is mostly, but not consistently, appropriate, fitting the 3-4 band top end.

The essay follows IB EE conventions: title page, abstract substitute, table of contents, headed sections, bibliography and appendices.  Flow is logical and aids comprehension.  Minor issues (some figures unlabelled or split, occasional page breaks cutting tables) do not seriously impair readability, matching the top of the 3-4 band.

Standard formatting featuresâ12-pt font, double spacing, in-text citations, bibliographyâare present.  Images are embedded and tables formatted clearly.  Page numbers appear in the extract and headings are consistent.  No word count is shown in the excerpt, so full compliance is uncertain; consequently a cautious 3 within the 3-4 band is awarded.

Arguments are logically structured (theory â data â implication â policy) and an explicit âLimitationsâ section recognises sampling issues and product heterogeneity.  Evaluation, however, remains somewhat superficial (e.g., limited critique of alternative explanations such as cost differences) and policy discussion is brief.  Thus the discussion is reasoned with partially critical evaluation, sitting mid-band 4-6.

The student processes data into unit prices, computes percentage differences and performs Welchâs t-tests, then links significance levels back to economic theory.  Analysis is clear and largely supported by evidence.  Depth is constrained by limited statistical robustness (small sample for each product) and minimal exploration of causality beyond stereotypes, but overall meets the top of the 4-6 band.

The essay demonstrates clear understanding of monopolistic competition, elasticity and third-degree price discrimination, employing diagrams and economic reasoning correctly.  Nonetheless, explanations occasionally rely on generic textbook statements and do not probe deeper, for example into welfare implications or alternative structures.  Knowledge is coherent but not exceptionally sophisticated, meriting the upper end of the 3-4 band.

Primary data set (324 observations across five major retailers) and multiple secondary studies are directly relevant to the research question; the majority of evidence aligns well with the investigationâs focus.  Some gaps exist (limited store types, no services data) but overall research is appropriate and clearly connected, consistent with the lower range of the 7-9 band.

The student explains a mixed-methods approach, distinguishes clearly between secondary foundation work and primary in-store price collection, justifies store and product selection, and applies Welchâs t-tests to compare unit prices.  Source selection criteria (recency, relevance) are mentioned and the range includes scholarly articles, government data and in-person observations.  While sampling is limited (five chains, 324 observations) and methodological weaknesses are acknowledged, the overall design is appropriate and shows informed selection of methods, aligning with the 5-6 band.

Economic terminology (elastic demand, consumer surplus, Welchâs t-test, third-degree price discrimination, monopolistic competition) is used accurately and consistently throughout text, tables and figures.  Occasional colloquial phrasing appears, but conceptual vocabulary remains precise, placing the work in the 5-6 band with a score of 5.

annotations.comment: The extended essay lacks a word count at the beginning, which should be added to comply with IB formatting requirements.
The research question is concise, unambiguous, and clearly tied to the investigationâs purpose.
Insert a brief discussion of how U.S. policy outcomes might inform Canadian legislative approaches.
The methodology section clearly outlines a mixedâmethods approach, linking theory, data collection, and analysis.
The description of foundational research lacks quantitative detail on source credibility and selection criteria.
Specify the sampling strategy (e.g., random, stratified) and sample size justification to strengthen methodological rigour.
The title effectively communicates the topic and focus, framing the investigation of gender-based price disparities clearly.
The table of contents is comprehensive; consider indenting subheadings to reflect the structure hierarchy more clearly.
Page numbers in the contents table are left-aligned; right-aligning them would improve readability.
Including a separate list of figures and tables after the contents could enhance navigation.
The introduction opens with a recent statistic that situates the study well in current research.
The final sentence in the introduction is incomplete; finish articulating how poverty context frames the significance.
The scope mentions Canada broadly; specifying âpersonal care productsâ early strengthens focus.
The background correctly identifies monopolistic competition, demonstrating clear understanding of market structure.
Expand on the welfare implications of price discrimination to deepen the analytical evaluation.
Figure 1 lacks axis labels and units; add descriptive labels in the caption for clarity.
The elasticity diagram effectively supports the theoretical description of demand responsiveness.
Inline math is inconsistently formatted; use $$ for display equations to maintain clarity.
Define the elasticity coefficient mathematically (e.g., $$\varepsilon=\tfrac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P}$$) to strengthen conceptual rigor.
Market segmentation is explained clearly with appropriate sourcing and terminology.
The discussion of gender stereotypes would benefit from integrating empirical sociological data to support claims.
Consider quantifying market shares by segment to reinforce the analysis of segmentation strategy.
The explanation of third-degree price discrimination is clear and uses correct economic terminology.
Citation of âTwin, 2024â omits page or section; ensure full citation details for credibility.
Discuss cost- or ingredient-based justifications (e.g., scent, formulation) to address counterarguments.
The inclusion of a New York City case study enhances comparative depth and context.
The link between U.S. legislative action and GTA context is underdeveloped; elaborate policy contrasts.
